text: The patient is positioned supine with arms tucked at the sides and legs straight out, supported in approximately twenty degrees Trendelenburg. The surgical robot is oriented with the patient's head on the opposite side from the foot.

The surgical team uses a four-port configuration with one assistant port in the patient's right lower quadrant. The camera, positioned between the left robotic arm and the assistant, is equipped with a thirty-degree down lens, which is adjusted to a thirty-degree up lens to view the anterior abdominal wall. Arm number three holds hot shears, while arm number four holds a ProGrasp. The ports are aligned approximately seven centimeters apart and positioned about four centimeters below the umbilicus, projecting upwards towards the patient's head.

The surgeon positions the patient in Trendelenburg to allow the small bowel and colon to fall cephalad, creating more space in the abdominal cavity. An incision is made starting at the cecum and extending to the ligament of Treitz, cutting through the posterior peritoneum. The cut edges of the posterior peritoneum are sutured to the abdominal wall to maintain exposure and keep the small bowel out of the surgical field.
The camera view shows the right iliac artery, providing orientation within the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and appendix, then makes an incision underneath the cecum through the posterior peritoneum. A flap is created and suspended to enhance exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon creates a flap by cutting along the edge of the posterior peritoneum beneath the cecum, working underneath the flap to enhance exposure.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps with a blunt tip for dissection, preferring them over the sharper Maryland dissector. The forceps are used to manipulate and dissect around a visible chord structure.

The surgeon creates a flap, leaving a deliberate thickness, while identifying the bowel, bowel mesentery, and a lymph node within the mesentery. The lymph node is visible but not of concern for the procedure.
The surgeon moves to the left side of the surgical field, where a cut edge is visible.

The surgeon identifies postganglionic nerve fibers originating from the hypogastric plexus, visible within the surgical field. Efforts are made to preserve these delicate nerve fibers during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the left common structure and uses suction to clear the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the lateral limit of the surgical field as the ureter. They examine a structure that may be the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) while noting the location of the common iliac artery.

The surgeon uses two needle drivers in arm one and arm three to position a long suspension suture. A 3-0 monofilament suture, similar to monocryl with an SH needle, is used to suspend the right cut edge upward.

The surgeon adjusts the positioning to improve exposure, ensuring the small bowel remains stable and does not fall into the surgical field. A Hemolock is used to secure structures, and the cecum is left intact to prevent the small bowel from shifting.

The surgeon continues the dissection to expose the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) on the left side, preparing to take the IMA for better exposure due to the presence of a left-sided tumor. Scissors and bipolar instruments are used for this part of the procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection cephalad, identifying the duodenum and a blue structure, which is likely the left renal vein or possibly the inferior vena cava, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies lymph node tissue and points out the renal vein, initially mistaking it for the inferior vena cava. The left renal vein is expected to be visible shortly.
The surgeon continues to define the left-sided flap, revealing the left ureter and its cord within the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) located on the side of the mesocolon, working underneath the left mesocolon in a distinct anatomical plane. A large lymphatic channel originating from the pancreas is visible, crossing over the left renal vein, and requires clipping.

The surgeon identifies a large lymphatic channel near the left renal vein, preparing to use a robotic clip to secure it and prevent chylous ascites. The lymphatic vessel is visibly prominent in the surgical field.
The surgeon applies another clip to the large lymphatic channels running over the left renal vein, ensuring better exposure of the area for precise placement.
The surgeon demonstrates the use of a robotic clip applier and hemlock scissors, highlighting their ability to achieve precise angles that may be challenging for the assistant to reach.
The surgeon prepares to use scissors for dissection, particularly when working underneath the aorta, highlighting the utility of this instrument in accessing challenging areas.
The surgeon identifies the gonadal vein and likely the lumbar vein in the surgical field, highlighting their anatomical positions.
The surgeon navigates around the renal artery, instructing the assistant to position instruments inside the surgical field for better access.

The surgeon identifies the left common iliac artery and the inferior mesenteric artery, preparing to control the IMA with clips. This maneuver aims to open up the surgical space, allowing access to the mass on the left side for removal.
The surgeon carefully dissects lymphatic tissue away from an artery, preparing to clip the artery for better access.

The surgeon identifies a large artery and applies Hemolox clips to secure it. After dividing the artery, the surgical space becomes more evident. The surgeon prefers to start the dissection from the bottom and move upwards, noting that taking the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) should not affect the colon's blood supply due to good collateral flow in young patients.
The surgeon identifies the general femoral nerve and begins dissecting around the cord, focusing on the left side of the aorta.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and the cord crossing over it, with adjacent tissue that may be perinephric fat or an artery. The discussion involves the choice between using clips or a robotic vessel sealer for ligation.

The surgeon identifies a vein near the area where the ureter crosses the iliac, marking the lower boundary of the node dissection. Lymphatic tissue is being dissected, while small branches are deemed unimportant for the procedure.
The surgeon pushes the iliac down, revealing the common iliac vein and a small branch extending from it.

The surgeon prepares to apply clips at the distal extent of the para-aortic nodes, clearly visible in the surgical field. The current scissors are deemed unsuitable, prompting a request for a sharper pair.
The surgeon points out the general femoral nerve and another adjacent white structure in the surgical field, highlighting their distinct appearances.

The surgeon identifies the sympathetic trunk, also referred to as the sympathetic chain, and prepares to follow it upwards. Using the DaVinci XI system, the surgeon adjusts the instruments to clip the structure, ensuring precise manipulation by pushing forward and pulling back as needed.
The surgeon uses suction to manage a small vessel bleeder that has appeared in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the lumbar sympathetic trunk and searches for postganglionic fibers, noting the small size of the fibers on the left side.
The surgeon identifies nerve trunks and the hypogastric plexus, located between the inferior vena cava and the aorta. The dissection continues on the left side.
The surgeon requests new scissors, which are handed over by an assistant named Brando.
The surgeon clips the left-sided fibers using a surgical clip, ensuring precise placement with sharp instruments.
The surgeon identifies a large postganglionic fiber extending from the sympathetic chain into the surgical field, with visible lymphatic structures nearby.
The surgeon prepares to divide lymphatic channels using clips to prevent spillage, focusing on the internal cable area. The decision is made to clip this side to avoid potential tumor spillage.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to separate the para-aortic lymph nodes from the interaortic cable and pre-aortic structures, carefully maneuvering to isolate these anatomical areas.
The surgeon identifies the right and left renal arteries adjacent to the surgical field, noting the absence of characteristics typical of a seminoma.

The surgeon uses a robotic clip in the right hand to secure a vessel, followed by the application of a vessel sealer to take the lumbar vessel. The surgical field shows visible lymphatic tissue, referred to as Kailash, in the area being operated on.
The surgeon uses scissors to address a lumbar vessel, followed by suctioning to assess the area for any issues. The surgical field is examined for potential complications.
The surgeon identifies the anterior spinous ligament and points out a cluster of lymphatics. The surgical team discusses clipping the lymphatics, with instruments visible in the field.
The surgeon carefully adjusts the angle of the instruments to avoid a nearby vein, preparing to take action on the targeted structure.
The surgeon uses suction to clear the surgical field, revealing the lumbar region and identifying the artery.
The surgeon exposes the gonadal vein and ensures perfect exposure of the surgical field. The ureter is noted to be far away from the operative area.

The surgeon uses suction to clear fluid from the surgical field, revealing a large lymphatic channel. The lymphatic tissue is visible, but the surgeon opts against using energy devices on it, preferring alternative methods.

The surgeon focuses on extending the dissection laterally, with assistance from Brando. The surgical field includes visible anatomical structures such as the anterior spinous ligament and lymphatic channels.
The surgeon identifies the artery and uses suction to clear the field, preparing to free the cord from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses suction to clear the surgical field, revealing large lymphatic channels and the cruciate diaphragm.
The surgeon performs a dissection, ensuring hemostasis and checking for lymphatic leakage in the surgical field before proceeding to another area.
The surgeon identifies the cord structure on one side, visually distinguishing it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon works on the cable side, identifying a small fiber and preparing to address a larger one. Suction is applied to maintain a clear surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a small lumbar fiber and instructs the assistant to adjust their position to ensure the fiber is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a small lumbar structure and requests assistance to push on the area, indicating the need for better exposure or manipulation.

The surgeon identifies three post-ganglionic trunks, which are small in size, and uses cold dissection to carefully trace and separate them from the surrounding lymphatics. The trunks are traced towards the hypogastric plexus, which is located directly above the lymphatic tissue.

The surgeon carefully navigates around the hypogastric plexus, identifying and cutting a trunk of nerves on the left side while avoiding nearby lymph nodes to prevent accidental severing of nerve trunks.
The surgeon works on the retro-aortic area, carefully dissecting and removing lymphatic tissue from the right side, which is visually easier to spare compared to the left side.
The surgeon identifies the lumbar region and prepares to remove it, noting a clip from the opposite side. The area potentially includes the cisterna chyli, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully manipulates and preserves a visible nerve bundle, ensuring its integrity while working on the current side of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses scissors to reveal a channel, exposing a prominent lymphatic vessel within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a clip to secure a significant lymphatic structure, referred to as the 'mother load,' while Brando assists by handing over the clip and removing the scissors from the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a grasper to manipulate tissue near the inter-aortic cable, ensuring nerve sparing while avoiding large trunks. The sigmoid colon is visible but not involved in the current procedure.
The surgeon identifies nerves to be preserved and points out a consistent vein prone to bleeding during the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon addresses a small bleeder under the cava, using suction to manage the bleeding. A large lymphatic structure is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer in the left hand to take the gonadal vessel and proceeds to free up the spermatic cord. The surgical field includes the aorta, which is visible during the procedure.

The surgeon has removed the right spermatic cord from the inguinal canal and is preparing to remove the left cord, navigating around the sigmoid colon. The completed lymph node dissections include paracable, intertacable, retrocable, retroaortic, and peraortic nodes, with visible nerve bundles preserved. There is noticeable bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects the gonadal vessels, which cross anterior to the ureter, ensuring to avoid the ureter as the gonadal vessels move laterally and the ureter moves medially.
The camera view shifts to reveal the dissection site from the opposite side, focusing on the exposed cord structure.

The surgeon navigates around the left hand limitation, successfully positioning underneath the vas deferens. A vasal vessel is observed with potential bleeding. The epigastric artery is visible, and deeper exploration is requested. Sutures are noted as the procedure transitions into an orchiectomy.
